(often abbreviated as **Johns Hopkins**, **Hopkins**, or **JHU**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland, United States. Founded in 1876 based on the European research institution model, Johns Hopkins is considered to be the first research university in the U.S.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

**Johns Hopkins University** (often abbreviated as **Johns Hopkins**, **Hopkins**, or **JHU**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland, United States. Founded in 1876 based on the European research institution model, Johns Hopkins is considered to be the first research university in the U.S.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The university was named for its first benefactor, the American entrepreneur and [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") philanthropist [Johns Hopkins](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins "Johns Hopkins").[[10]](#cite_note-There_is_only_one_Johns_Hopkins-10) Hopkins's $7 million bequest (equivalent to $166 million in 2024)[[11]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-11) to establish the university and the affiliated [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital") in Baltimore was the largest [philanthropic](/wiki/Philanthropy "Philanthropy") gift in U.S. history up to that time.[[12]](#cite_note-Facts_at_a_Glance-12)[[13]](#cite_note-racial_record-13) [Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman"), who was inaugurated as [Johns Hopkins's first president](/wiki/Category:Presidents_of_Johns_Hopkins_University "Category:Presidents of Johns Hopkins University") on February 22, 1876,[[14]](#cite_note-Inaugural_Address_of_Daniel_Coit_Gilman-14) led the university to revolutionize higher education in the U.S. by integrating teaching and research.[[15]](#cite_note-Benson_Page_x-15) In 1900, Johns Hopkins became a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").[[16]](#cite_note-16) The university has led all [U.S. universities](/wiki/Higher_education_in_the_United_States "Higher education in the United States") in annual research and development expenditures for over four consecutive decades.[[17]](#cite_note-June-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine"), established in 1893, has achieved international recognition for its pioneering biomedical research.

The university consists of ten academic divisions mostly divided among four campuses in Baltimore, with some graduate campuses in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[19]](#cite_note-19) The university's two undergraduate divisions, the [Zanvyl Krieger School of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Zanvyl_Krieger_School_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Zanvyl Krieger School of Arts and Sciences") and the [Whiting School of Engineering](/wiki/Whiting_School_of_Engineering "Whiting School of Engineering"), are located on the [Homewood campus](/wiki/Homewood_Campus_of_Johns_Hopkins_University "Homewood Campus of Johns Hopkins University") adjacent to Baltimore's [Charles Village](/wiki/Charles_Village,_Baltimore "Charles Village, Baltimore") neighborhood.[[20]](#cite_note-homewoodcampus-20) The [School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine"), [School of Nursing](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Nursing "Johns Hopkins School of Nursing"), and [Bloomberg School of Public Health](/wiki/Bloomberg_School_of_Public_Health "Bloomberg School of Public Health") are located on the medical campus in East Baltimore, alongside the [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital").[[21]](#cite_note-eastbaltimorecampus-21) The university also consists of the [Peabody Institute](/wiki/Peabody_Institute "Peabody Institute") in Baltimore's [Mount Vernon](/wiki/Mount_Vernon,_Baltimore "Mount Vernon, Baltimore") neighborhood, [Applied Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Applied Physics Laboratory") in [Howard County](/wiki/Howard_County,_Maryland "Howard County, Maryland"), [School of Advanced International Studies](/wiki/School_of_Advanced_International_Studies "School of Advanced International Studies"), [School of Education](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Education "Johns Hopkins School of Education"), and [Carey Business School](/wiki/Carey_Business_School "Carey Business School").[[22]](#cite_note-hopkinscampuses-22)

Founded in 1883, the [Blue Jays men's lacrosse](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Blue_Jays_men%27s_lacrosse "Johns Hopkins Blue Jays men's lacrosse") team, which is an affiliate member in the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"), has won 44 national titles.[[23]](#cite_note-Johns_Hopkins_Fact_Book-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Johns_Hopkins_men's_lacrosse_program_to_join_Big_Ten-24) The university's other sports teams compete in [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") of the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association"), where they are members of the [Centennial Conference](/wiki/Centennial_Conference "Centennial Conference").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Philanthropic beginnings and foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Philanthropic beginnings and foundation")]

Further information: [Humboldtian model of higher education](/wiki/Humboldtian_model_of_higher_education "Humboldtian model of higher education") and [Johns Hopkins](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins "Johns Hopkins")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Hopkinsp.jpg/250px-Hopkinsp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hopkinsp.jpg)

[Johns Hopkins](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins "Johns Hopkins"), the university's namesake whose philanthropic gift in 1873 established the university, [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital"), and the [Johns Hopkins School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Heidelberg_Universit%C3%A4tsbibliothek_2003_b.jpg/250px-Heidelberg_Universit%C3%A4tsbibliothek_2003_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_Universit%C3%A4tsbibliothek_2003_b.jpg)

The university model of [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University") in [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg "Heidelberg"), Germany, was replicated in the founding of Johns Hopkins University

On his death in 1873, [Johns Hopkins](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins "Johns Hopkins"), a [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") entrepreneur and childless bachelor, bequeathed $7 million (equivalent to $166 million in 2024)[[11]](#cite_note-inflation-USGDP-11) to fund a hospital and university in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore").[[25]](#cite_note-HC-25)

At the time, this donation, generated primarily from the [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad"),[[26]](#cite_note-Who_Was_Johns_Hopkins?-26) was the largest philanthropic gift in the history of the United States,[[12]](#cite_note-Facts_at_a_Glance-12) and endowment was then the largest in America.[[13]](#cite_note-racial_record-13) Until 2020, Hopkins was assumed to be a fervent [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism"), until research done by the school into his [United States Census](/wiki/United_States_Census "United States Census") records revealed he claimed to own at least five household slaves in the 1840 and 1850 decennial censuses.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The first name of philanthropist Johns Hopkins comes from the surname of his great-grandmother, Margaret Johns, who married Gerard Hopkins.[[26]](#cite_note-Who_Was_Johns_Hopkins?-26) They named their son Johns Hopkins, who named his own son Samuel Hopkins. Samuel named one of his sons for his father, and that son became the university's benefactor. [Milton Eisenhower](/wiki/Milton_Eisenhower "Milton Eisenhower"), a former university president, once spoke at a convention in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") where the [master of ceremonies](/wiki/Master_of_ceremonies "Master of ceremonies") introduced him as "President of *John* Hopkins". Eisenhower retorted that he was "glad to be here in *Pitt*burgh".[[29]](#cite_note-Cheesecake_on_the_Tart_Side-29)

The original board opted for an entirely novel university model dedicated to the discovery of knowledge at an advanced level, extending that of contemporary Germany.[[30]](#cite_note-muller-30) Building on the [Humboldtian model of higher education](/wiki/Humboldtian_model_of_higher_education "Humboldtian model of higher education"), the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") education model of [Wilhelm von Humboldt](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Humboldt "Wilhelm von Humboldt"), it became dedicated to research. It was especially [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University") and its long academic research history on which the new institution tried to model itself.[[30]](#cite_note-muller-30)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Johns Hopkins thereby became the model of the modern research university in the United States. Its success eventually shifted higher education in the United States from a focus on teaching revealed and/or applied knowledge to the scientific discovery of new knowledge.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

[Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has the text of a [1911 *Encyclopædia Britannica*](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition") article about [the Early History](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/1911_Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica/Johns_Hopkins_University "wikisource:1911 Encyclopædia Britannica/Johns Hopkins University").

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

Further information: [Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman"), [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital"), [Johns Hopkins School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine"), and [Johns Hopkins University Press](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_Press "Johns Hopkins University Press")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Daniel_Coit_Gilman1.jpg/250px-Daniel_Coit_Gilman1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daniel_Coit_Gilman1.jpg)

[Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman"), the first president of Johns Hopkins University

The trustees worked alongside four notable university presidents, [Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot") of [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), [Andrew D. White](/wiki/Andrew_Dickson_White "Andrew Dickson White") of [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), [Noah Porter](/wiki/Noah_Porter "Noah Porter") of [Yale College](/wiki/Yale_College "Yale College"), and [James B. Angell](/wiki/James_Burrill_Angell "James Burrill Angell") of [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan"). They each supported [Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman") to lead the new university and he became the university's first president.[[32]](#cite_note-PH-32) Gilman, a [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University")-educated scholar, had been serving as president of the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") prior to this appointment.[[32]](#cite_note-PH-32) In preparation for the university's founding, [Daniel Coit Gilman](/wiki/Daniel_Coit_Gilman "Daniel Coit Gilman") visited [University of Freiburg](/wiki/University_of_Freiburg "University of Freiburg") and other German universities.

Gilman launched what many at the time considered an audacious and unprecedented academic experiment to merge teaching and research. He dismissed the idea that the two were mutually exclusive: "The best teachers are usually those who are free, competent and willing to make original researches in the library and the laboratory," he stated.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Hopkins_Hall%2C_1885.jpg/250px-Hopkins_Hall%2C_1885.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hopkins_Hall,_1885.jpg)

Hopkins Hall on the original [Downtown Baltimore](/wiki/Downtown_Baltimore "Downtown Baltimore") campus, c. 1885

To implement his plan, Gilman recruited internationally known researchers including the mathematician [James Joseph Sylvester](/wiki/James_Joseph_Sylvester "James Joseph Sylvester"); the biologist [H. Newell Martin](/wiki/H._Newell_Martin "H. Newell Martin"); the physicist [Henry Augustus Rowland](/wiki/Henry_Augustus_Rowland "Henry Augustus Rowland"), the first president of the [American Physical Society](/wiki/American_Physical_Society "American Physical Society"), the [classical scholars](/wiki/Classical_scholars "Classical scholars") [Basil Gildersleeve](/wiki/Basil_Gildersleeve "Basil Gildersleeve"), and Charles D. Morris;[[34]](#cite_note-university-34) the economist [Richard T. Ely](/wiki/Richard_T._Ely "Richard T. Ely"); and the chemist [Ira Remsen](/wiki/Ira_Remsen "Ira Remsen"), who became the second president of the university in 1901.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Gilman focused on the expansion of graduate education and support of faculty research. The new university fused advanced scholarship with such professional schools as medicine and engineering. Hopkins became the national trendsetter in [doctoral](/wiki/PhD "PhD") programs and the host for numerous scholarly journals and associations.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The [Johns Hopkins University Press](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_Press "Johns Hopkins University Press"), founded in 1878, is the oldest American [university press](/wiki/University_press "University press") in continuous operation.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital%2C_early_photo.jpg/250px-Johns_Hopkins_Hospital%2C_early_photo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johns_Hopkins_Hospital,_early_photo.jpg)

[Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital"), c. 1880s–1890s

With the completion of [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital") in 1889 and the [medical school](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine") in 1893, the university's research-focused mode of instruction soon began attracting world-renowned faculty members who would become major figures in the emerging field of academic medicine, including [William Osler](/wiki/William_Osler "William Osler"), [William Halsted](/wiki/William_Halsted "William Halsted"), [Howard Kelly](/wiki/Howard_Atwood_Kelly "Howard Atwood Kelly"), and [William Welch](/wiki/William_H._Welch "William H. Welch").[[38]](#cite_note-38) During this period the university further made history by becoming the first medical school to admit women on an equal basis with men and to require a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), based on the efforts of [Mary E. Garrett](/wiki/Mary_Garrett "Mary Garrett"), who had endowed the school at Gilman's request.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The [school of medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine") was America's first coeducational, graduate-level medical school, and became a prototype for academic medicine that emphasized bedside learning, research projects, and laboratory training.

In his will and in his instructions to the trustees of the university and the hospital, Hopkins requested that both institutions be built upon the vast grounds of his Baltimore estate, Clifton. When Gilman assumed the presidency, he decided that it would be best to use the university's endowment for recruiting faculty and students, deciding to, as it has been paraphrased, "build men, not buildings."[[40]](#cite_note-40) In his will Hopkins stipulated that none of his endowment should be used for construction; only interest on the principal could be used for this purpose. Unfortunately, stocks in The [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad"), which would have generated most of the interest, became virtually worthless soon after Hopkins's death. The university's first home was thus in Downtown Baltimore, delaying plans to site the university in Clifton.[[25]](#cite_note-HC-25)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Further information: [Applied Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Applied Physics Laboratory"), [School of Advanced International Studies](/wiki/School_of_Advanced_International_Studies "School of Advanced International Studies"), [Peabody Institute](/wiki/Peabody_Institute "Peabody Institute"), and [Whiting School of Engineering](/wiki/Whiting_School_of_Engineering "Whiting School of Engineering")

In the early 20th century, the university outgrew its buildings and the trustees began to search for a new home. Developing Clifton for the university was too costly, and 30 acres (12 ha) of the estate had to be sold to the city as public park. A solution was achieved by a team of prominent locals who acquired the estate in north Baltimore known as the [Homewood Estate](/wiki/Homewood_Campus_of_Johns_Hopkins_University "Homewood Campus of Johns Hopkins University"). On February 22, 1902, this land was formally transferred to the university. The flagship building, Gilman Hall, was completed in 1915. The [School of Engineering](/wiki/Whiting_School_of_Engineering "Whiting School of Engineering") relocated in fall of 1914 and the [Zanvyl Krieger School of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Zanvyl_Krieger_School_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Zanvyl Krieger School of Arts and Sciences") followed in 1916. These decades saw the ceding of lands by the university for the public Wyman Park and Wyman Park Dell and the [Baltimore Museum of Art](/wiki/Baltimore_Museum_of_Art "Baltimore Museum of Art"), coalescing in the contemporary area of 140 acres (57 ha).[[25]](#cite_note-HC-25)

Prior to becoming the main Johns Hopkins campus, the Homewood estate had initially been the gift of Charles Carroll of [Carrollton, Maryland](/wiki/Carrollton,_Maryland "Carrollton, Maryland"), a planter and signer of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), to his son Charles Carroll Jr. The original structure, the 1801 [Homewood House](/wiki/Homewood_Museum "Homewood Museum"), still stands and serves as an on-campus museum.[[41]](#cite_note-HH-41) The brick and marble [Federal](/wiki/Federal_architecture "Federal architecture") style of Homewood House became the architectural inspiration for much of the university campus versus the [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") style of other historic American universities.[[41]](#cite_note-HH-41)

In 1909, the university was among the first to start adult [continuing education](/wiki/Continuing_education "Continuing education") programs and in 1916 it founded the nation's first [school of public health](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Bloomberg_School_of_Public_Health "Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health").[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Since the 1910s, Johns Hopkins University has famously been a "fertile cradle" to [Arthur Lovejoy](/wiki/Arthur_Lovejoy "Arthur Lovejoy")'s [history of ideas](/wiki/History_of_ideas "History of ideas").[[43]](#cite_note-Paulson1970-43)

Since 1942, the [Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory (APL)](/wiki/Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Applied Physics Laboratory") has served as a major governmental defense contractor. In tandem with on-campus research, Johns Hopkins has every year since 1979 had the highest federal research funding of any American university.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Professional schools of international affairs and music were established in 1950 and 1977, respectively, when the [School of Advanced International Studies](/wiki/School_of_Advanced_International_Studies "School of Advanced International Studies")[[45]](#cite_note-45) in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and the [Peabody Institute](/wiki/Peabody_Institute "Peabody Institute")[[46]](#cite_note-46) in Baltimore were incorporated into the university.

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

Further information: [Carey Business School](/wiki/Carey_Business_School "Carey Business School") and [Johns Hopkins School of Education](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Education "Johns Hopkins School of Education")

The early decades of the 21st century saw expansion across the university's institutions in both physical and population sizes. Notably, a planned 88-acre expansion to the medical campus began in 2013.[[47]](#cite_note-Medical_Campus_Expansion-47) Completed construction on the [Homewood campus](/wiki/Homewood_campus "Homewood campus") has included a new [biomedical engineering](/wiki/Biomedical_engineering "Biomedical engineering") building in the [Johns Hopkins University Department of Biomedical Engineering](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_Department_of_Biomedical_Engineering "Johns Hopkins University Department of Biomedical Engineering"), a new library, a new biology wing, an extensive renovation of the flagship Gilman Hall, and the reconstruction of the main university entrance.[[48]](#cite_note-Charles_Street_Reconstruction-48)

These years also brought about the rapid development of the university's professional schools of education and business. From 1999 until 2007, these disciplines had been joined within the School of Professional Studies in Business and Education (SPSBE), itself a reshuffling of several earlier ventures. The 2007 split, combined with new funding and leadership initiatives, has led to the simultaneous emergence of the [Johns Hopkins School of Education](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Education "Johns Hopkins School of Education") and the [Carey Business School](/wiki/Carey_Business_School "Carey Business School").[[49]](#cite_note-new_professional_schools-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Legg_mason_tower.jpg/250px-Legg_mason_tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:Legg_mason_tower.jpg)

[Legg Mason Tower](/wiki/Legg_Mason_Tower "Legg Mason Tower"), home of the new [Carey Business School](/wiki/Carey_Business_School "Carey Business School")

On November 18, 2018, it was announced that [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") would make a donation to his alma mater of $1.8 billion, marking the largest private donation in modern history to an institution of [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") and bringing Bloomberg's total contribution to the school in excess of $3.3 billion.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Bloomberg's $1.8 billion gift allows the school to practice [need-blind admission](/wiki/Need-blind_admission "Need-blind admission") and meet the full financial need of admitted students.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In January 2019, the university announced[[58]](#cite_note-58) an agreement to purchase the [Newseum](/wiki/Newseum "Newseum"), located at 555 Pennsylvania Ave. NW, in the heart of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), with plans to locate all of its Washington, D.C.–based graduate programs there. In an interview with *[The Atlantic](/wiki/The_Atlantic "The Atlantic")*, the president of Johns Hopkins stated that, "the purchase is an opportunity to position the university, literally, to better contribute its expertise to national- and international-policy discussions."[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In late 2019, the university's Coronavirus Research Center began tracking worldwide cases of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") by compiling data from hundreds of sources around the world.[[60]](#cite_note-EW-60) This led to the university becoming one of the most cited sources for data about the pandemic.[[60]](#cite_note-EW-60)

In November 2025, following the $1.8 billion gift from alumnus Michael Bloomberg in 2018, the university announced tuition-free plans, for current students in the spring 2026 semester and for new enrollees in the 2026-27 year who were from families earning less than $200,000, in support of making education available to all students based on merit and not means, and citing U.S. Census data showing that around 85% of American households would be eligible. For students from families earning under $100,000, additional aid would cover tuition, fees and living expenses, thus requiring no support from their families.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

#### Johns Hopkins Police Department

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Johns Hopkins Police Department")]

In 2019, Johns Hopkins sought and received permission from the [Maryland General Assembly](/wiki/Maryland_General_Assembly "Maryland General Assembly") to create a private police force to patrol in and around the three Baltimore campuses.[[62]](#cite_note-62) The proposal generated opposition from students, faculty, and surrounding community associations.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-neighborhoods-map-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-inside-HE-concerns-66) A resolution expressing concerns and raising questions about accountability and oversight of the proposed police department was unanimously passed by the Homewood Faculty Assembly.[[67]](#cite_note-pugh-corruption-garland-67) Protests culminated in a month-long sit-in and building occupation at Garland Hall, the university's administrative building, which ended after Baltimore Police surrounded the building and arrested the participants in May 2019.[[68]](#cite_note-garland-arrest-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Following the murder of George Floyd in 2020, thousands of faculty, staff, students, and community members petitioned the university to reconsider the department.[[70]](#cite_note-70) In June 2020, university president Ronald J. Daniels announced a pause of at least two years in the development and implementation of the department.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

As part of the agreement with the university on authorizing the police force, the Maryland General Assembly required the creation of a civilian accountability board and a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with the Baltimore Police Department. A draft MOU was released for public comment in September 2022 amidst continued protest and criticism regarding the university's engagement with the community.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) The finalized MOU, issued in December 2022, grants the Johns Hopkins Police Department primary jurisdiction over university-controlled property and adjacent public areas.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Officer recruitment and training began in spring 2024, with active patrols commencing in summer 2024.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Second Trump Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second Trump Administration")]

Beginning in January 2025, federal funding cuts by the Trump administration's [Department of Government Efficiency](/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency "Department of Government Efficiency") targeting the [U.S. Agency for International Development](/wiki/United_States_Agency_for_International_Development "United States Agency for International Development") (USAID) caused Johns Hopkins to lose $800 million in grants and forced the university to lay off thousands of employees and cancel research projects.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In September, the university reported a total decline in research grants awarded by the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health") (NIH) by approximately 40% compared to the previous year and the termination of more than 80 existing grants, reducing the value of the university's research portfolio by $500 million.[[80]](#cite_note-80) In response to these challenges, the university imposed a hiring freeze on new staff members, limited discretionary spending, and indefinitely paused wage increases for employees. After the Trump administration announced it would impose a cap of 15% on indirect or "overhead" research costs, which would reduce the university's research funding by $300 million annually, Johns Hopkins joined 40 other universities in a successful lawsuit seeking to reverse the change.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

In February 2025, [Leo Terrell](/wiki/Leo_Terrell "Leo Terrell"), the head of the Trump administration's [Task Force to Combat Antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_the_United_States#Efforts_to_combat_antisemitism "Antisemitism in the United States"), announced that he would investigate Johns Hopkins University as part of the Department of Justice's broader investigation into [antisemitism on college campuses](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_US_higher_education "Antisemitism in US higher education").[[82]](#cite_note-82) In July 2025, [America First Legal](/wiki/America_First_Legal "America First Legal") founder, the Trump adviser and [white nationalist](/wiki/White_nationalism "White nationalism") [Stephen Miller](/wiki/Stephen_Miller "Stephen Miller"), requested that the assistant attorney general [Harmeet K Dhillon](/wiki/Harmeet_Dhillon "Harmeet Dhillon") and the justice department investigate "illegal" diversity, equity, and inclusion practices and "a discriminatory DEI regime as a core institutional mandate" in the university.

### Civil rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Civil rights")]

#### African-Americans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: African-Americans")]

Hopkins was a prominent [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") who supported [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). After his death, reports said his conviction was a decisive factor in enrolling Hopkins's first [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") student, [Kelly Miller](/wiki/Kelly_Miller_(scientist) "Kelly Miller (scientist)"), a graduate student in physics, astronomy and mathematics.[[83]](#cite_note-mdhistoryonline-83) As time passed, the university adopted a "separate but equal" stance more like other Baltimore institutions.[[13]](#cite_note-racial_record-13)

The first black undergraduate entered the school in 1945 and graduate students followed in 1967.[[84]](#cite_note-timeline_JHSPH-84) James Nabwangu, a British-trained Kenyan, was the first black graduate of the medical school.[[85]](#cite_note-In_a_Sea_of_White_Faces-85) African-American instructor and laboratory supervisor [Vivien Thomas](/wiki/Vivien_Thomas "Vivien Thomas") was instrumental in developing and conducting the first successful [blue baby operation](/wiki/Blue_baby_syndrome "Blue baby syndrome") in 1944.[[86]](#cite_note-86) Despite such cases, racial diversity did not become commonplace at Johns Hopkins institutions until the 1960s and 1970s.

#### Women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Women")]

Hopkins's most well-known battle for women's rights was the one led by daughters of trustees of the university; [Mary E. Garrett](/wiki/Mary_Garrett "Mary Garrett"), [M. Carey Thomas](/wiki/M._Carey_Thomas "M. Carey Thomas"), Mamie Gwinn, Elizabeth King, and Julia Rogers.[[87]](#cite_note-women-87) They donated and raised the funds needed to open the medical school, and required Hopkins's officials to agree to their stipulation that women would be admitted. The [nursing school](/wiki/Nursing_school "Nursing school") opened in 1889 and accepted women and men as students.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Other graduate schools were later opened to women by president [Ira Remsen](/wiki/Ira_Remsen "Ira Remsen") in 1907. [Christine Ladd-Franklin](/wiki/Christine_Ladd-Franklin "Christine Ladd-Franklin") was the first woman to earn a PhD at Hopkins, in mathematics in 1882.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The trustees denied her the degree for decades and refused to change the policy about admitting women. In 1893, Florence Bascomb became the university's first female PhD.[[87]](#cite_note-women-87) The decision to admit women at undergraduate level was not considered until the late 1960s and was eventually adopted in October 1969. As of 2009–2010, the undergraduate population was 47% female and 53% male.[[90]](#cite_note-Johns_Hopkins_University-90) In 2020, the undergraduate population of Hopkins was 53% female.[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

#### Freedom of speech

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Freedom of speech")]

On September 5, 2013, cryptographer and Johns Hopkins university professor [Matthew Green](/wiki/Matthew_D._Green "Matthew D. Green") posted a blog entitled, "On the NSA", in which he contributed to the ongoing debate regarding the role of [NIST](/wiki/NIST "NIST") and [NSA](/wiki/NSA "NSA") in formulating U.S. [cryptography](/wiki/Cryptography "Cryptography") standards. On September 9, 2013, Green received a take-down request for the "On the NSA" blog from interim Dean Andrew Douglas from the Johns Hopkins University [Whiting School of Engineering](/wiki/Whiting_School_of_Engineering "Whiting School of Engineering").[[93]](#cite_note-GuardianOnTakeDown-93) The request cited concerns that the blog had links to sensitive material. The blog linked to already published news articles from *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, and [ProPublica.org](/wiki/ProPublica.org "ProPublica.org"). Douglas subsequently issued a personal on-line apology to Green.[[94]](#cite_note-DaraKerrOnApology-94) The event raised concern over the future of academic freedom of speech within the cryptologic research community.

### Homewood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Homewood")]

Main article: [Homewood Campus of Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Homewood_Campus_of_Johns_Hopkins_University "Homewood Campus of Johns Hopkins University")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/View_from_Levering_Plaza.jpg/250px-View_from_Levering_Plaza.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_Levering_Plaza.jpg)

View of Gilman Hall from the Levering Plaza on the Homewood Campus

For its first two decades of existence, university was based in Downtown Baltimore. In the early 20th century, the trustees acquired the Homewood estate of Charles Carroll, son of [Charles Carroll of Carollton](/wiki/Charles_Carroll_of_Carrollton "Charles Carroll of Carrollton"), a signer of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"). Carroll's [Homewood House](/wiki/Homewood_House "Homewood House") is considered one of the finest examples of [Federal architecture](/wiki/Federal_architecture "Federal architecture"), which most of the university's buildings are modeled after. Most undergraduate programs are on the Homewood Campus.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

* [Whiting School of Engineering](/wiki/Whiting_School_of_Engineering "Whiting School of Engineering"): The Whiting School contains 14 undergraduate and graduate engineering programs and 12 additional areas of study.[[96]](#cite_note-Departments_&_Areas_of_Study-96)
* [Zanvyl Krieger School of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Zanvyl_Krieger_School_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Zanvyl Krieger School of Arts and Sciences"): The Krieger School offers more than 60 undergraduate majors and minors and more than 40 graduate programs.[[97]](#cite_note-Krieger_School_of_Arts_&_Sciences-97)

The [School of Education](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Education "Johns Hopkins School of Education") has a building at the southern edge of the Homewood Campus, adjacent to Wyman Park Dell.

### East Baltimore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: East Baltimore")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d4/Hopkins_hospital.jpg/250px-Hopkins_hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hopkins_hospital.jpg)

[The Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital"), located in the university's medical campus in East Baltimore

Collectively known as Johns Hopkins Medical Institutions (JHMI) campus, the East Baltimore facility occupies several city blocks spreading from the [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital") trademark dome.

* [Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Bloomberg_School_of_Public_Health "Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health"): The Bloomberg School was founded in 1916 and is the world's oldest and largest school of public health. It has consistently been ranked first in its field by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.
* [School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine"): The School of Medicine is widely regarded as one of the best medical schools and [biomedical research](/wiki/Biomedical_research "Biomedical research") institutes in the world.
* [School of Nursing](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Nursing "Johns Hopkins School of Nursing"): The School of Nursing is one of America's oldest and pre-eminent schools for nursing education. It has consistently ranked first in the nation.

## Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Organization")]

The Johns Hopkins entity is structured as two corporations, the university and The Johns Hopkins Health System, formed in 1986. The president is JHU's chief executive officer and the university is organized into nine academic divisions.[[105]](#cite_note-overview-105)

JHU's bylaws specify a board of trustees of between 18 and 65 voting members. Trustees serve six-year terms subject to a two-term limit. The alumni select 12 trustees. Four recent alumni serve 4-year terms, one per year, typically from the graduating class. The bylaws prohibit students, faculty or administrative staff from serving on the board, except the president as an ex-officio trustee.[[106]](#cite_note-Bylaws-106) The Johns Hopkins Health System has a separate board of trustees, many of whom are doctors or health care executives.[[107]](#cite_note-Johns_Hopkins_Medicine_Adds_Four_to_Board-107)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Academics")]

The full-time, four-year undergraduate program is "most selective" with low transfer-in and a high graduate co-existence.[[108]](#cite_note-Carnegie-108) The Princeton Review rates the selectivity of Johns Hopkins as 99/99. As of 2025, the cost of attendance per academic year without financial aid is approximately $89,000.[[109]](#cite_note-109) However, 51% of full-time undergraduates receive financial aid covering 100% of their need.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The admit rate of Hopkins undergraduates to medical school is 80% and to law school is 97%, some of the highest rates in the US.[[111]](#cite_note-GF-111) The university is one of fourteen founding members of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") (AAU); it is also a member of the [Consortium on Financing Higher Education](/wiki/Consortium_on_Financing_Higher_Education "Consortium on Financing Higher Education") (COFHE) and the [Universities Research Association](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association") (URA).

The university's undergraduate programs are highly selective: in 2021, the Office of Admissions accepted about 4.9% of its 33,236 Regular Decision applicants [[142]](#cite_note-142) and about 6.4% of its total 38,725 applicants.[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) In 2022, 99% of admitted students graduated in the top 10% of their high school class.[[111]](#cite_note-GF-111) Over time, applications to Johns Hopkins University have risen steadily; as a result, the selectivity of Johns Hopkins University has also increased. [Early Decision](/wiki/Early_Decision "Early Decision") I is an option at Johns Hopkins University for students who wish to demonstrate that the university is their first choice. These students, if admitted, are required to enroll. This application is due November 1. There is also another binding Early Decision II application due January 3. Many students, however, apply Regular Decision, which is a traditional non-binding round. These applications are due January 3 and students are notified in mid-March. The cost to apply to Hopkins is $70, though fee waivers are available. In 2014, Johns Hopkins ended legacy preference in admissions.[[146]](#cite_note-146) Johns Hopkins practices [need-blind admission](/wiki/Need-blind_admission "Need-blind admission") and meets the full financial need of all admitted students.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Libraries")]

Further information: [George Peabody Library](/wiki/George_Peabody_Library "George Peabody Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/George-peabody-library.jpg/250px-George-peabody-library.jpg)](/wiki/File:George-peabody-library.jpg)

The [George Peabody Library](/wiki/George_Peabody_Library "George Peabody Library") at Johns Hopkins University

The Johns Hopkins University Library system houses more than 3.6 million volumes[[156]](#cite_note-American_Library_Association_Fact_Sheet-156) and includes ten main divisions across the university's campuses. The largest segment of this system is the Sheridan Libraries, encompassing the [Milton S. Eisenhower Library](/wiki/Milton_S._Eisenhower_Library "Milton S. Eisenhower Library") (the main library of the [Homewood campus](/wiki/Homewood_campus "Homewood campus")), the Brody Learning Commons, the Hutzler Reading Room ("The Hut") in Gilman Hall, the John Work Garrett Library at [Evergreen House](/wiki/Evergreen_House "Evergreen House"), and the [George Peabody Library](/wiki/George_Peabody_Library "George Peabody Library") at the [Peabody Institute](/wiki/Peabody_Institute "Peabody Institute") campus.[[157]](#cite_note-157)

The main library, constructed in the 1960s, was named for [Milton S. Eisenhower](/wiki/Milton_S._Eisenhower "Milton S. Eisenhower"), former president of the university and brother of former U.S. president [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower"). The university's stacks had previously been housed in Gilman Hall and departmental libraries.[[158]](#cite_note-gil-158) Only two of the Eisenhower library's six stories are above ground, though the building was designed so that every level receives natural light. The design accords with campus lore that no structure can be taller than Gilman Hall, the flagship academic building. A four-story expansion to the library, known as the Brody Learning Commons, opened in August 2012. The expansion features an energy-efficient, state-of-the-art technology infrastructure and includes study spaces, seminar rooms, and a rare books collection.[[159]](#cite_note-JHU_News_Releases-159)

### Johns Hopkins University Press

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Johns Hopkins University Press")]

Main article: [Johns Hopkins University Press](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_Press "Johns Hopkins University Press")

The Johns Hopkins University Press is the publishing division of the Johns Hopkins University. It was founded in 1878 and holds the distinction of being the oldest continuously running [university press](/wiki/University_press "University press") in the United States.[[160]](#cite_note-About_the_Press-160) To date the Press has published more than 6,000 titles and currently publishes 65 scholarly periodicals and over 200 new books each year. Since 1993, the Johns Hopkins University Press has run [Project MUSE](/wiki/Project_MUSE "Project MUSE"), an online collection of over 250 full-text, peer-reviewed journals in the humanities and social sciences. The Press also houses the Hopkins Fulfilment Services (HFS), which handles distribution for a number of university presses and publishers. Taken together, the three divisions of the Press—Books, Journals (including MUSE) and HFS—make it one of the largest of America's university presses.

### Center for Talented Youth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Center for Talented Youth")]

Main article: [Center for Talented Youth](/wiki/Center_for_Talented_Youth "Center for Talented Youth")

The Johns Hopkins University also offers the [Center for Talented Youth](/wiki/Center_for_Talented_Youth "Center for Talented Youth") program, a nonprofit organization dedicated to identifying and developing the talents of the most promising K-12 grade students worldwide. As part of the Johns Hopkins University, the "Center for Talented Youth" or CTY helps fulfill the university's mission of preparing students to make significant future contributions to the world.[[161]](#cite_note-161) The Johns Hopkins Digital Media Center (DMC) is a multimedia lab space as well as an equipment, technology and knowledge resource for students interested in exploring creative uses of emerging media and use of technology.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

### Degrees offered

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Degrees offered")]

Johns Hopkins offers a number of degrees in various undergraduate majors leading to the BA and BS and various majors leading to the MA, MS and PhD for graduate students.[[163]](#cite_note-163) Because Hopkins offers both undergraduate and graduate areas of study, many disciplines have multiple degrees available. [Biomedical engineering](/wiki/Biomedical_engineering "Biomedical engineering"), perhaps one of Hopkins's best-known programs, offers bachelor's, master's, and doctoral degrees.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Research")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/New_Horizons_LORRI.jpg/250px-New_Horizons_LORRI.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Horizons_LORRI.jpg)

Installing a [New Horizons](/wiki/New_Horizons "New Horizons") imager at Johns Hopkins University's [Applied Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Applied Physics Laboratory") in [Laurel, Maryland](/wiki/Laurel,_Maryland "Laurel, Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/15-150-NasaTeam-NewHorizonsCallsHomeAfterPlutoFlyby-20150714.jpg/250px-15-150-NasaTeam-NewHorizonsCallsHomeAfterPlutoFlyby-20150714.jpg)](/wiki/File:15-150-NasaTeam-NewHorizonsCallsHomeAfterPlutoFlyby-20150714.jpg)

View of Mission Operations at the Applied Physics Laboratory in Laurel, Maryland

The opportunity to participate in important research is one of the distinguishing characteristics of Hopkins's undergraduate education. About 80 percent of undergraduates perform independent research, often alongside top researchers.[[108]](#cite_note-Carnegie-108)[[165]](#cite_note-Johns_Hopkins_News_Release_–_JHU-165) In fiscal year 2020, Johns Hopkins spent nearly $3.1 billion on research, more than any other U.S. university for over 40 consecutive years.[[17]](#cite_note-June-17) Johns Hopkins has had seventy-seven members of the [Institute of Medicine](/wiki/Institute_of_Medicine "Institute of Medicine"), forty-three [Howard Hughes Medical Institute](/wiki/Howard_Hughes_Medical_Institute "Howard Hughes Medical Institute") Investigators, seventeen members of the [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering"), and sixty-two members of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences"). As of March 2025, 34 Nobel Prize winners have been affiliated with the university as alumni, faculty members or researchers, with the most recent winners being [Gregg Semenza](/wiki/Gregg_L._Semenza "Gregg L. Semenza") and [William G. Kaelin](/wiki/William_Kaelin_Jr. "William Kaelin Jr.").[[166]](#cite_note-The_Johns_Hopkins_University:_Nobel_Prize_Winners-166)

Between 1999 and 2009, Johns Hopkins was among the most cited institutions in the world. It attracted nearly 1,222,166 citations and produced 54,022 papers under its name, ranking third globally after [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") and the [Max Planck Society](/wiki/Max_Planck_Society "Max Planck Society") in the number of *total* citations published in Thomson Reuters-indexed journals over 22 fields in America.[[167]](#cite_note-ScienceWatch.com-167) In 2020, Johns Hopkins University ranked 5 in number of utility patents granted out of all institutions in the world.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

In 2000, Johns Hopkins received $95.4 million in research grants from the [National Aeronautics and Space Administration](/wiki/National_Aeronautics_and_Space_Administration "National Aeronautics and Space Administration") (NASA), making it the leading recipient of [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") funding.[[169]](#cite_note-JHU_NASA_R&D_2000-169) In FY 2002, Hopkins became the first university to cross the $1 billion threshold on either list, recording $1.14 billion in total research and $1.023 billion in federally sponsored research. In FY 2008, Johns Hopkins University performed $1.68 billion in science, medical and engineering research, making it the leading U.S. academic institution in total R&D spending for the 30th year in a row, according to a [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") (NSF) ranking.[[170]](#cite_note-ascribe_oct09_r&d-170) These totals include grants and expenditures of JHU's Applied Physics Laboratory in Laurel, Maryland.

In 2013, the [Bloomberg Distinguished Professorships](/wiki/Bloomberg_Distinguished_Professorships "Bloomberg Distinguished Professorships") program was established by a $250 million gift from [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg"). This program enables the university to recruit fifty researchers from around the world to joint appointments throughout the nine divisions and research centers. Each professor must be a leader in [interdisciplinary](/wiki/Interdisciplinary "Interdisciplinary") research and be active in [undergraduate education](/wiki/Undergraduate_education "Undergraduate education").[[171]](#cite_note-anderson-171)[[172]](#cite_note-172) Directed by Vice Provost for Research [Denis Wirtz](/wiki/Denis_Wirtz "Denis Wirtz"), there are currently thirty two Bloomberg Distinguished Professors at the university, including three [Nobel Laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"), eight fellows of the [American Association for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/American_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "American Association for the Advancement of Science"), ten members of the [American Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences"), and thirteen members of the National Academies.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

## Student life

[Charles Village](/wiki/Charles_Village,_Baltimore "Charles Village, Baltimore"), the region of North Baltimore surrounding the university, has undergone several restoration projects, and the university has gradually bought the property around the school for additional student housing and dormitories. *The Charles Village Project*, completed in 2008, brought new commercial spaces to the neighborhood. The project included Charles (now Scott-Bates) Commons, a new, modern residence hall that includes popular retail franchises.[[182]](#cite_note-Charles_Commons-185)[[183]](#cite_note-einsteinbros-186) In 2015, the university began development of new commercial properties, including a modern upperclassmen apartment complex, restaurants and eateries, and a CVS retail store.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

Hopkins invested in improving campus life with an arts complex in 2001, the Mattin Center, and a three-story sports facility, the O'Connor Recreation Center. The large on-campus dining facilities at Homewood were renovated in the summer of 2006. The Mattin Center was demolished in 2021 to make room for the new Student Center scheduled to open in the fall of 2024.

Quality of life is enriched by the proximity of neighboring academic institutions, including [Loyola College](/wiki/Loyola_College_in_Maryland "Loyola College in Maryland"), [Maryland Institute College of Art (MICA)](/wiki/Maryland_Institute_College_of_Art "Maryland Institute College of Art"), [UMBC](/wiki/UMBC "UMBC"), [Goucher College](/wiki/Goucher_College "Goucher College"), and [Towson University](/wiki/Towson_University "Towson University"), as well as the nearby neighborhoods of [Hampden](/wiki/Hampden,_Baltimore "Hampden, Baltimore"), the [Inner Harbor](/wiki/Inner_Harbor "Inner Harbor"), [Fells Point](/wiki/Fells_Point "Fells Point"), and [Mount Vernon](/wiki/Mount_Vernon,_Baltimore "Mount Vernon, Baltimore").

Students and alumni are active on and off campus. Johns Hopkins has been home to several [secret societies](/wiki/Secret_society "Secret society"), many of which are now [defunct](/wiki/List_of_defunct_Johns_Hopkins_University_societies "List of defunct Johns Hopkins University societies"). Blue Jay Supper Society is the only active secret society with open applications.[[185]](#cite_note-188) Membership is open to undergraduate and graduate students as well as alumni.

### Fraternity and sorority life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Fraternity and sorority life")]

[Fraternity and sorority](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities") life came to Hopkins in 1876 with the chartering of [Beta Theta Pi](/wiki/Beta_Theta_Pi "Beta Theta Pi") fraternity, which still exists on campus today.[[186]](#cite_note-189) As of Spring 2025, Johns Hopkins is home to seven social fraternities and five social sororities, as well as ten culturally-based sororities and fraternities.[[187]](#cite_note-Recognized_Organizations-190) The seven social fraternities at Hopkins all belong to the national [North-American Interfraternity Conference](/wiki/North_American_Interfraternity_Conference "North American Interfraternity Conference") and are locally governed by the Interfraternity Council (IFC). The five social sororities at Hopkins belong to the [National Panhellenic Conference](/wiki/National_Panhellenic_Conference "National Panhellenic Conference") and are locally governed by the Panhellenic Association at Johns Hopkins.[[187]](#cite_note-Recognized_Organizations-190) [Alpha Phi Alpha](/wiki/Alpha_Phi_Alpha "Alpha Phi Alpha"), a historically black fraternity, was founded in 1991, [Lambda Phi Epsilon](/wiki/Lambda_Phi_Epsilon "Lambda Phi Epsilon"), an Asian-interest fraternity, was founded in 1994, and [Lambda Upsilon Lambda](/wiki/Lambda_Upsilon_Lambda "Lambda Upsilon Lambda"), a Latino-interest fraternity, was founded in 1995.[[188]](#cite_note-191)[[189]](#cite_note-192)[[190]](#cite_note-193) Rush for all students occurs in the spring. Most fraternities keep houses in the nearby [Charles Village](/wiki/Charles_Village,_Baltimore "Charles Village, Baltimore") or [Oakenshawe](/wiki/Oakenshawe_Historic_District "Oakenshawe Historic District") neighborhoods, while sororities do not.

### Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Traditions")]

While it has been speculated that Johns Hopkins has relatively few traditions for a school of its age and that many past traditions have been forgotten, a handful of myths and customs are ubiquitous knowledge among the community.[[191]](#cite_note-traditions-194) One such long-standing myth surrounds the university seal that is embedded into the floor of the Gilman Hall foyer. The myth holds that any current student to step on the seal will never graduate. In reverence for this tradition, the seal has been fenced off from the rest of the room.

An annual winter event is the "Lighting of the Quads", a ceremony each winter during which the campus is lit up in holiday lights. Recent years have included singing and fireworks.

The Spring Fair has been a Johns Hopkins tradition since 1972 and has since grown to be the largest student-run festival in the country.[[192]](#cite_note-195) Popular among Hopkins students and Baltimore inhabitants alike, the Spring Fair features carnival rides, vendors, food and a [beer garden](/wiki/Beer_garden "Beer garden"). Since its beginning, Spring the fair has decreased in size, both in regard to attendance and utilization of space.[[193]](#cite_note-196)

### Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Housing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/AMR_I%2C_JHU.jpg/250px-AMR_I%2C_JHU.jpg)](/wiki/File:AMR_I,_JHU.jpg)

Alumni Memorial Residence I, a freshman dormitory on the [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") campus

Living on campus is typically required for first- and second-year undergraduates.[[194]](#cite_note-Johns_Hopkins_Freshman_Room_Selection_Brochure-197) Freshman housing is centered around Freshman Quad, which consists of three residence hall complexes: The two Alumni Memorial Residences (AMR I and AMR II) plus Buildings A and B. The AMR dormitories are each divided into *houses*, subunits named for figures from the university's early history. Freshmen are also housed in Wolman Hall and in certain wings of McCoy Hall, both located slightly outside the campus. Dorms at Hopkins are generally co-ed with same-gender rooms, though a new policy has allowed students to live in mixed-gender rooms since Fall 2014.[[195]](#cite_note-Gender_Inclusive_Housing-198)[[196]](#cite_note-Freshman_Residence_Halls-199)

Students determine where they will live during sophomore year through a housing lottery. Sophomores in university housing occupy one of four buildings: McCoy Hall, the Bradford Apartments, the Homewood Apartments, and Scott-Bates Commons.[[197]](#cite_note-Freshman_Room_Selection-200)

Most juniors and seniors move into nearby apartments or row-houses. Most are located in the neighboring [Charles Village](/wiki/Charles_Village "Charles Village") community. Forty-five percent of the student body lives off-campus while 55% lives on campus.[[198]](#cite_note-201)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Johns Hopkins Blue Jays](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Blue_Jays "Johns Hopkins Blue Jays")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Hopkins_athletics_wmark.png/250px-Hopkins_athletics_wmark.png)](/wiki/File:Hopkins_athletics_wmark.png)

Johns Hopkins athletics wordmark

The university's athletic teams are the [Johns Hopkins Blue Jays](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Blue_Jays "Johns Hopkins Blue Jays"). Even though [sable](/wiki/Sable_(heraldry) "Sable (heraldry)") and [gold](/wiki/Or_(heraldry) "Or (heraldry)") are used for [academic robes](/wiki/Academic_dress "Academic dress"), the university's athletic colors are spirit blue (PMS 284) and [black](/wiki/Black "Black").[[199]](#cite_note-202) Hopkins celebrates [Homecoming](/wiki/Homecoming "Homecoming") in the spring to coincide with the height of the [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse") season. The men's and women's lacrosse teams are in [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA) [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") and are affiliate members of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"). Other teams are in [Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") and participate in the [Centennial Conference](/wiki/Centennial_Conference "Centennial Conference").[[200]](#cite_note-203) JHU is also home to the [Lacrosse Museum and National Hall of Fame](/wiki/Lacrosse_Museum_and_National_Hall_of_Fame "Lacrosse Museum and National Hall of Fame"), maintained by [US Lacrosse](/wiki/US_Lacrosse "US Lacrosse").[[201]](#cite_note-204)

The school's most prominent team is its [men's lacrosse team](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Blue_Jays_men%27s_lacrosse "Johns Hopkins Blue Jays men's lacrosse"). The team has won 44 [national titles](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_lacrosse_tournament "NCAA Division I men's lacrosse tournament"),[[202]](#cite_note-205) nine [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") titles in 2007, 2005, 1987, 1985, 1984, 1980, 1979, 1978, and 1974, and 29 [USILA](/wiki/United_States_Intercollegiate_Lacrosse_Association "United States Intercollegiate Lacrosse Association") championships, and six Intercollegiate Lacross Association (ILA) titles.

As the first American university dedicated towards a research mission, Johns Hopkins was among the earliest institutions in the U.S. to grant doctorates and trained many scholars who later became leaders in their fields,[[204]](#cite_note-207) including biologist [Thomas Hunt Morgan](/wiki/Thomas_Hunt_Morgan "Thomas Hunt Morgan"), who received the [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") for identifying chromosomes as the mechanical basis of heredity;[[205]](#cite_note-208) [pragmatist](/wiki/Pragmatism "Pragmatism") philosopher and psychologist [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey");[[206]](#cite_note-209) [Florence Bascom](/wiki/Florence_Bascom "Florence Bascom"), considered the "first woman geologist in America";[[207]](#cite_note-210) mathematician [John Charles Fields](/wiki/John_Charles_Fields "John Charles Fields"), the namesake of [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"); historian [Frederick Jackson Turner](/wiki/Frederick_Jackson_Turner "Frederick Jackson Turner"), who developed the "[frontier thesis](/wiki/Frontier_Thesis "Frontier Thesis")"; [theoretical physicist](/wiki/Theoretical_physics "Theoretical physics") [John A. Wheeler](/wiki/John_Archibald_Wheeler "John Archibald Wheeler"), who was credited with coining the term "[black hole](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole")";[[208]](#cite_note-211) and Civil War historian [James M. McPherson](/wiki/James_M._McPherson "James M. McPherson").

The [School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine"), founded in 1893, served as a model for modern medical education and is considered one of the leading centers for medical research and innovation in the United States.[[209]](#cite_note-212) Its notable graduates include cardiothoracic surgeon [Denton Cooley](/wiki/Denton_Cooley "Denton Cooley"), who performed the first [total artificial heart](/wiki/Artificial_heart "Artificial heart") implantation; biotechnologist and inventor [Leroy "Lee" Hood](/wiki/Leroy_Hood "Leroy Hood"); "father of [medical genetics](/wiki/Medical_genetics "Medical genetics")" [Victor McKusick](/wiki/Victor_A._McKusick "Victor A. McKusick"); pioneering brain surgeon [Wilder Penfield](/wiki/Wilder_Penfield "Wilder Penfield"); [Dorothy Reed Mendenhall](/wiki/Dorothy_Reed_Mendenhall "Dorothy Reed Mendenhall"), who discovered the [Reed-Sternberg cells](/wiki/Reed%E2%80%93Sternberg_cell "Reed–Sternberg cell") that characterize [Hodgkin lymphoma](/wiki/Hodgkin_lymphoma "Hodgkin lymphoma"); and [Rochelle Walensky](/wiki/Rochelle_Walensky "Rochelle Walensky"), [CDC Director](/wiki/Centers_for_Disease_Control_and_Prevention "Centers for Disease Control and Prevention") during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). [Modernist](/wiki/Literary_modernism "Literary modernism") poet and novelist [Gertrude Stein](/wiki/Gertrude_Stein "Gertrude Stein") spent four years at the [Johns Hopkins School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins School of Medicine"), but left before completing her degree.[[210]](#cite_note-213)

Many Hopkins alumni have entered politics and government. Alumni in the [119th Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress") include Representatives [Lauren Underwood](/wiki/Lauren_Underwood "Lauren Underwood"), [Andy Harris](/wiki/Andy_Harris_(politician) "Andy Harris (politician)"), [Sara Elfreth](/wiki/Sarah_Elfreth "Sarah Elfreth"), and [Kweisi Mfume](/wiki/Kweisi_Mfume "Kweisi Mfume"). Four [U.S. Senators](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), [Daniel Brewster](/wiki/Daniel_Brewster "Daniel Brewster"), [George L.P. Radcliffe](/wiki/George_L._P._Radcliffe "George L. P. Radcliffe"), and [John Marshall Butler](/wiki/John_Marshall_Butler "John Marshall Butler") of Maryland and [Rudy Boschwitz](/wiki/Rudy_Boschwitz "Rudy Boschwitz") of Minnesota have studied at Hopkins.

[Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson"), the first and only [U.S. President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") to hold a [doctoral degree](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate"), received his Ph.D. in history from Johns Hopkins in 1890.[[211]](#cite_note-214) 39th [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") and Governor of Maryland (1967–1969) [Spiro Agnew](/wiki/Spiro_Agnew "Spiro Agnew") studied chemistry at Hopkins.[[212]](#cite_note-215) Cabinet-level officials who attended Johns Hopkins include [Secretary of War](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_War "United States Secretary of War") [Newton D. In athletics, Johns Hopkins is primarily known for its long and historically dominant lacrosse tradition. Many of the most influential figures in the early history of the sport played at Hopkins, including the namesakes of the NCAA's [Schmeisser Award](/wiki/Schmeisser_Award "Schmeisser Award") for defensemen and [Jack Turnbull Award](/wiki/Jack_Turnbull_Award "Jack Turnbull Award") for best attackman. More recent graduates of the men's lacrosse program include [Premier Lacrosse League](/wiki/Premier_Lacrosse_League "Premier Lacrosse League") founder [Paul Rabil](/wiki/Paul_Rabil "Paul Rabil") and [Tewaaraton Award](/wiki/Tewaaraton_Award "Tewaaraton Award") winner[Kyle Harrison](/wiki/Kyle_Harrison_(lacrosse) "Kyle Harrison (lacrosse)"), both considered among the greatest players of their generation. In other sports, [Wes Unseld Jr.](/wiki/Wes_Unseld_Jr. "Wes Unseld Jr."), assistant coach for the [NBA's](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls") and former [Washington Wizards](/wiki/Washington_Wizards "Washington Wizards") head coach, played college basketball at Hopkins.[[223]](#cite_note-226)

### Nobel laureates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Nobel laureates")]

Main article: [List of Nobel laureates affiliated with Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_affiliated_with_Johns_Hopkins_University "List of Nobel laureates affiliated with Johns Hopkins University")

As of March 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit), there have been 34 Nobel Laureates affiliated with Johns Hopkins as students, faculty, or researchers.[[224]](#cite_note-WinnersList-227)[[225]](#cite_note-228)[[226]](#cite_note-229) [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson"), who received his [PhD](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") from Johns Hopkins in 1886, was the university's first affiliated laureate, winning the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") in 1919.[[224]](#cite_note-WinnersList-227)[[227]](#cite_note-Peace1919-230)

Eighteen Johns Hopkins affiliates have won the [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine").[[224]](#cite_note-WinnersList-227) Four Nobel Prizes were shared by Johns Hopkins laureates: [George Minot](/wiki/George_Minot "George Minot") and [George Whipple](/wiki/George_Whipple "George Whipple") won the 1934 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine,[[228]](#cite_note-Medicine1934-231) [Joseph Erlanger](/wiki/Joseph_Erlanger "Joseph Erlanger") and [Herbert Spencer Gasser](/wiki/Herbert_Spencer_Gasser "Herbert Spencer Gasser") won the 1944 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine,[[229]](#cite_note-Medicine1944-232) [Daniel Nathans](/wiki/Daniel_Nathans "Daniel Nathans") and [Hamilton O. Smith](/wiki/Hamilton_O._Smith "Hamilton O. Smith") won the 1978 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine,[[230]](#cite_note-Medicine1978-233) and [David H. Hubel](/wiki/David_H._Hubel "David H. Hubel") and [Torsten N. Wiesel](/wiki/Torsten_N._Wiesel "Torsten N. Wiesel") won the 1981 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.[[231]](#cite_note-Medicine1981-234)

Four Johns Hopkins affiliates have won Nobel Prizes in Physics: [James Franck](/wiki/James_Franck "James Franck") in 1925, [Maria Goeppert-Mayer](/wiki/Maria_Goeppert_Mayer "Maria Goeppert Mayer") in 1963, [Riccardo Giacconi](/wiki/Riccardo_Giacconi "Riccardo Giacconi") in 2002,[[232]](#cite_note-Physics_2002-235) [Bloomberg Distinguished Professor](/wiki/Bloomberg_Distinguished_Professorships "Bloomberg Distinguished Professorships") [Adam Riess](/wiki/Adam_Riess "Adam Riess") in 2011.[[233]](#cite_note-Physics_2011-236)

[Bloomberg Distinguished Professor](/wiki/Bloomberg_Distinguished_Professorships "Bloomberg Distinguished Professorships") [Peter Agre](/wiki/Peter_Agre "Peter Agre") was awarded the 2003 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") (which he shared with [Roderick MacKinnon](/wiki/Roderick_MacKinnon "Roderick MacKinnon")) for his discovery of [aquaporins](/wiki/Aquaporins "Aquaporins").[[234]](#cite_note-237) Bloomberg Distinguished Professor [Carol Greider](/wiki/Carol_Greider "Carol Greider") was awarded the 2009 [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") for Physiology or Medicine, along with [Elizabeth Blackburn](/wiki/Elizabeth_Blackburn "Elizabeth Blackburn") and [Jack W. Szostak](/wiki/Jack_W._Szostak "Jack W. Szostak"), for their discovery that telomeres are protected from progressive shortening by the enzyme telomerase.[[235]](#cite_note-238)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johns_Hopkins_University&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The school's reputation has made it a frequent reference in media.

* *The Hopkins Lacrosse Story* (1992): With an unprecedented 43 national championship titles, Johns Hopkins has one of the most successful [college lacrosse](/wiki/College_lacrosse "College lacrosse") programs in the world. This [documentary film](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") traces the team's numerous historical accomplishments: its first championship in 1891, its wins at the [Amsterdam (1928)](/wiki/1928_Summer_Olympics "1928 Summer Olympics") and [Los Angeles (1932)](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") Olympic Games, and the current runs for the NCAA title.[[236]](#cite_note-239)
* *Hopkins 24/7* (2000): A six-part [television documentary](/wiki/Television_documentary "Television documentary") produced by [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") that gave viewers an inside look at life in the [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital").[[237]](#cite_note-UC-240)
* *[Something the Lord Made](/wiki/Something_the_Lord_Made "Something the Lord Made")* (2004): An [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") movie that tells the story of an unusual partnership at [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital") between [Alfred Blalock](/wiki/Alfred_Blalock "Alfred Blalock"), one of the nation's pioneering surgeons, and [Vivien Thomas](/wiki/Vivien_Thomas "Vivien Thomas"), an [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") surgical technician, who contributed to a surgical solution for the "blue baby" syndrome. It was filmed on the East Baltimore and Homewood campuses.[[237]](#cite_note-UC-240)
* *[Hopkins](/wiki/Hopkins_(TV_series) "Hopkins (TV series)")* (2008): A seven-part documentary series on the [Johns Hopkins Hospital](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Hospital "Johns Hopkins Hospital") produced by ABC shows the real life dramas taking place there each day for doctors, nurses, residents, and patients.[[237]](#cite_note-UC-240)
